00
10
15

**F‑01 — Who can reset passwords?** (§3.3 vs §4.5)
Admin only 
Counter will recommend creating a new account if admin is not available 

**F‑02 — Who can adjust a locked‑in price?** (§6.3 vs §6.5)
Counter and admin can adjust prices, kitchen cannot  

**F‑03 — First‑admin bootstrap vs grant‑time credential mandates** (§4.3)
Your assumption with /setup is fine. Please keep it 

**F‑04 — Who can close a sitting?** (§5.4 vs §4.3)
That's right, both counter and admin can close a sitting. 

**F‑05 — Counter role has no credential requirement** (§4.4)
Counter needs same as kitchen 

**F‑06 — TLS / secure context never addressed; quick tunnels break passkeys.** *(largest technical gap)*
TLS needs to be from cloudflare wither quick tunnels or permanent tunnels. 
We did a proof of concept of this with our golang project at https://github.com/kusl/gotunnels 
Go Tunnels is publicly available on github 
It has
    - Passkeys (WebAuthn) as the primary login, a password fallback (argon2id), and optional TOTP two-factor (RFC 6238, works with Aegis / Google Authenticator), with single-use recovery codes.
    - Server-side sessions using an opaque Bearer token that works cross-origin (the frontend and API are on different tunnel domains). The token lives in localStorage, so a session persists across tabs and browser restarts and never expires on its own — you stay signed in until you explicitly log out (a deliberate anti–password-fatigue choice; tunable via GOTUNNELS_SESSION_TTL). Settings includes "log out everywhere" to revoke every session across all devices at once.
    - Privacy-preserving audit log: client IPs are never stored raw — only sha256(pepper || ip), and the hash is shown to you plainly on your own activity page.
    - A shared plain-text notes feed (create / delete, never edit) with visibility-aware polling for live updates, deferred "new notes" while you read, and server-side ownership checks in the SQL itself.
    - A CAPTCHA toy with server-backed stats: the auto-solver can do hundreds of solves a second, so the browser batches deltas and syncs every few seconds; a RANK() OVER leaderboard and a per-user preferences endpoint round it out.
    - OpenTelemetry traces, metrics, and logs over OTLP/HTTP — vendor-neutral, never tied to a specific backend.
    - Content-Security-Policy in report-only mode by default; violations are reported back to the API, stored, and logged to telemetry.
    - No exposed host ports; everything talks over the internal Compose network by service name.
Unlike this demo, OUR argon2 parameters need to be ROBUST 

**F‑07 — Order cardinality.** 
Guests can edit their order at any time until closed by the counter 
Kitchen can mark individual items as fulfiled 
but guests are free to order more 
every single guest change should send a notification to the kitchen 

**F‑08 — "TOTP enabled" vs "TOTP required" conflated.** (§4.2)
TOTP if present is always required. 
TOTP is always required if password is present for admins, counter, and kitchen staff 
Admin can reset password for anyone which will also remove TOTP 
Users are required to create a new password after loggin in the first time after a password reset 

**F‑09 — Admin sign‑in UX under a literal reading of §4.3.**
Yes, that's right. admin may sign in with password + totp 

**F‑10 — Post‑close edit window undefined.** (§5.5, §6.5)
yes, orders are read only post close 

**F‑10b — "Delete any user" vs append‑only history.** (§4.3)
yes, deletes are soft deletes 

**F‑11 — Attribution granularity toward guests.** (§6.5)
yes, guests see role but we still save individual logins for auditability 

**F‑12 — Static, never‑rotated QR is a permanent capability token.** (§5.1)
This is a good catch. 
While an existing guest approving a QR code is a good mitigation, 
we have opened a vulnerability through which anyone can flood the system 
from anywhere in the world opening a new session 
every time the counter closes a session by a bad actor. 
we need to somehow require the people to be at the table 
which means the qr code must update periodically while the table is "open" 
you may not use an older qr code to sign in, only the current qr code
so this yet yet another web site / web page and yet another role 
because we can't have the kitchen or counter be logged in on a display at every single table
also we don't know if there will be two tables or twenty 
counter needs to be able to "close" tables 
this is getting complicated pretty fast 

**F‑13 — Redis has no v1 consumer.** (§2)
Not having redis if we don't need it is ok 

**F‑14 — Aspire vs Podman Compose overlap.** (§2)
Get rid of aspire. Prefer podman compose. 

**F‑15 — `UPTRACE_DSN` is not a "standard OpenTelemetry environment variable".** (§2)
This is an optional environment variable and should require no code change. 
A working demo is at https://github.com/kusl/GoTunnels/blob/main/scripts/up.sh 

**F‑16 — Backup mechanics underspecified.** (§7.x)
Thank you. Yes, please implement these in the shell script

**F‑17 — `run.sh` invoked but never defined.** (§1)
It should do something similar to this 
https://github.com/kusl/GoTunnels/blob/main/scripts/run.sh
#   1. regenerates the LLM context dump (export.sh), if present
#   2. runs the full test suite (build + vet + unit tests)
#   3. builds the images and brings the whole stack up, printing the two
#      Cloudflare Quick Tunnel URLs

**F‑18 — Price‑change timelines promised but never recorded.** (§4.3 vs §6)
Yes, this is correct as well 

**F‑19 — Assorted unspecified operational details.**
Currency, time zone, username policy, lockout policy, and whether anonymous ordering exists are all silent. *Spec defaults:* ISO 4217 `RESTAURANT_CURRENCY_CODE`; host time zone; case‑insensitive usernames 3–64 chars (`citext`); lockout 5 failures / 5 minutes plus endpoint rate limiting; **no anonymous ordering** — every guest authenticates (the requirements imply this but never state it).
thankyou, accepted 

**F‑20 — Moq exclusion rationale is factually off.** (§2 testing notes)
Yes, please correct this in the documentation when we write the code / adr 
actually, prefer testing with a "real" postgresql database that we stand up and tear down as necessary 
if we build this project correctly with SOLID principles, we should have very few tests that REQUIRE mocking. 

**F‑21 — "Three physical roles" vs four applications.** 
and there is a new one in table. Please square this circle in the documentation as well. 

**F‑22 — The no‑abbreviations rule collides with mandated abbreviations.** (§8)
Please update as necessary to use full names everywhere 

**F‑23 — Hardcoded personal path in the example.** (§1 vs §8)
yes, please make it generic 

**F‑24 — A "Resolved:" bullet inside the "not yet decided" list.** (§10)
I removed it 

**F‑25 — `export.sh` is emitted twice.**
Fixed. I added it to the exclude list

**F‑26 — `REQUIREMENTS.md` is absent from the dump.** 
Fixed. I moved it up to docs from docs/llm 

**F‑27 - 33 
Please review the latest export.sh fix all the defects and include a new one in your output 










































































/
Claude finished the response

pasted

please review the dump.txt

Presented file
Done
All rulings are folded in. Here's everything, in three parts.

Dump verification. F-25 and F-26 both landed: export.sh now appears exactly once (via its self-documentation section) and REQUIREMENTS.md is present — the dump carries all three docs at commit ca9eee1, clean tree, coherent metadata. Two cosmetic artifacts of the old script are still visible in it: the doubled . root above the file tree (F-28) and yarn.lock in the exclusion banner (F-32). Both disappear the first time you regenerate with the new script.

Rulings. F-01–05, F-09–11, F-13–19, F-21–24 are recorded as stated and will be reflected verbatim in the next doc revision. Four need a word back from you — I've picked defaults so a silent "fine" is a valid answer:

F-06: Cloudflare tunnels it is, with one sharp edge I verified: trycloudflare.com is on the Public Suffix List, so on quick tunnels a passkey binds to the random per-run subdomain and dies with it. Durable passkeys therefore require a named tunnel on your stable domain; quick tunnels stay fine for demos with password+TOTP. I'll also carry your "ROBUST Argon2" instruction into a custom IPasswordHasher (Argon2id, ~64 MiB / t=3 / p=1, configurable), and note the accepted risk that in-house ordering hairpins through Cloudflare, so LAN ordering depends on WAN health. I'm taking GoTunnels as the tunnel/TLS/observability reference only — its never-expiring localStorage bearer session doesn't fit Blazor Server's cookie/circuit model.
F-07 (Q1): I'll respec as one living order per guest per sitting: edits staged client-side and sent explicitly, each send is one batch event and one kitchen alert (not per-keystroke, to avoid alert fatigue); lines get a pending → fulfilled lifecycle replacing order-level Acknowledged; guests may remove only not-yet-fulfilled lines. Veto if you want literal per-change notifications.
F-08 (Q2/Q3): Default: TOTP is challenged on password sign-ins only, not passkey sign-ins (the authenticator is already a second factor). Admin reset wipes password+TOTP; next sign-in forces new password, then immediate TOTP re-enrollment before anything else. The per-user "admin TOTP toggle" from §4.2 disappears.
F-12 (Q4/Q5): New role table_display with surface /display/{table} — a cheap per-table device paired once via an admin one-time code, showing a QR that encodes {origin}/table/{id}?token=HMAC(table_secret, time_window), rotating every 60 s, current+previous window accepted, rotation independent of sitting state; counter can read the current code off their own screen if a display dies. Q5: I'm speccing rotating-only — printed static QRs are gone entirely rather than kept as a flagged fallback. Veto if you want the fallback documented.
F-20's Moq rationale will be corrected (2023 SponsorLink incident, not licensing; and it's FluentAssertions v8 that went commercial — the free fork is AwesomeAssertions), with tests preferring a real PostgreSQL stood up and torn down. F-17's run.sh will mirror GoTunnels' three-phase script.

export.sh — F-27–33 fixed, six new defects found. The rewrite is at the link above; all seven originals are closed: atomic write via temp-file + rename with the console echoing the finished file (F-27), single tree root (F-28), redundant redirection removed (F-29), bc replaced with awk (F-30), a correct recursive fallback tree with proper └──/├──/│ connectors (F-31), yarn.lock gone and the script now self-excludes by its own runtime name so a rename can't resurrect F-25 (F-32), singular/plural file-count noun (F-33). While rewriting I found six more for DOCUMENTATION_REVIEW.md:

F-34 — Binary detection keyed everything on file(1); without it, every file was misclassified binary and the dump contained zero content. Now MIME is primary with a NUL-byte sniff of the first 8 KiB as fallback.
F-35 — 2>&1 folded stderr diagnostics into the dump itself; stderr now stays on the console.
F-36 — The trailing-newline guard compared $(tail -c1) to $'\n', but command substitution strips trailing newlines, so every properly-terminated file gained a spurious blank line. Now counts newlines in the final byte.
F-37 — sort ran under the ambient locale, making dump ordering machine-dependent. Pinned LC_ALL=C (matches git's byte order; tree --charset=UTF-8 keeps the connectors pretty).
F-38 — Staged-but-never-committed files printed an empty "Last git commit:"; now (not yet committed).
F-39 — date --iso-8601 is GNU-only while stat had BSD fallbacks; timestamps now have the same fallback posture.
Testing: byte-identical console/dump.txt, clean stderr, both tree branches exercised (tree 2.1.1 and the pure-bash fallback), a stripped-PATH run without file(1)/tree(1) (PNG still detected binary, text intact), empty file, no-trailing-newline file, filename with spaces, staged-only file, one-file and zero-file repos, rename self-exclusion, foreign-cwd invocation, silent non-repo exit, and ShellCheck 0.11.0 with zero findings. One behavior fix found in testing: mktemp creates 0600, so the script now restores 0644 before the rename.

When you've confirmed or vetoed Q1–Q5, the next atomic pass is the updated REQUIREMENTS.md + TECHNICAL_SPECIFICATION.md + the ADR set (including new ADRs for rotating table tokens and Argon2id, and revisions for Aspire removal, Cloudflare TLS, and the order model).


Export
SH 

Want to be notified when Claude responds?






Claude is AI and can make mistakes. Please double-check responses.
